definition: A directed flow of charges (in metals: free electrons) through a conductor. Current strength is measured in amperes (A) and indicates how many charges pass a point per second.
The electrical force that drives the current, measured in volts (V). Voltage is energy per charge – a 9 V battery gives 9 joules of energy to every coulomb of charge that passes through it.

The conductor's opposition to the flow of current, measured in ohms (Ω). Resistance depends on the material, and the length and thickness of the conductor – a longer and thinner conductor has higher resistance.
The relationship U = R · I, where U is voltage (V), R is resistance (Ω) and I is current (A). The law describes how voltage, current and resistance are related in an electrical circuit.
Components connected one after another in a single loop. The current is the same everywhere in the circuit. If one bulb fails, they all go out.
Components connected in separate branches with the same voltage. If one bulb fails, the others continue to shine. The lamps in a home are connected in parallel.
A material that conducts electric current well because of free electrons. Metals such as copper and aluminium are typical conductors and are used in cables and wires.
A material that does not conduct electric current. Plastic, rubber, glass and dry air are insulators and are used to protect conductors and prevent unwanted current flow.
The rate at which electrical energy is converted, P = U · I, measured in watts (W). A 2000 W kettle converts 50 times more energy per second than a 40 W bulb.
The unit of electrical energy (kWh) used on electricity bills. 1 kWh equals a power of 1,000 W for 1 hour. A 2000 W kettle running for 30 minutes consumes 1 kWh.
A protective component that breaks the circuit if the current exceeds a set value. Protects against overheating and fire risk in the event of a short circuit or overload.
A safety device that trips at the slightest leakage current to earth – for example if someone accidentally touches a live part. It reacts much faster than an ordinary fuse.
